data.title: Wednesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The Mortgage Bankers' Association compiles various mortgage loan indexes. The purchase applications index measures applications at mortgage lenders.

8:30 AM Empire State Manufacturing Index

The New York Fed conducts this monthly survey of manufacturers in New York State. Participants from across the state represent various industries.

Manufacturing is considered sluggish, with the index barely in contraction territory at minus 2.0 for April, down from an already unimpressive minus 0.2 in March.

8:30 AM Import and Export Prices

Import price indexes are compiled for the prices of goods that are bought in the United States but produced abroad, and export price indexes are compiled for the prices of goods sold abroad but produced domestically.

The consensus sees rising fuel costs lifting import prices by 2.1 percent and export prices by 1.7 percent on the month!

 8:30 AM Michael Barr Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr participates in a discussion on "Consumer Compliance Supervision and Regulation" before the National Community Reinvestment Coalition (NCRC) Just Economy Conference.

10:00 AM Housing Market Index

The housing market index is a monthly composite that tracks home builder assessments of present and future sales as well as buyer traffic. 

Homebuilder sentiment remains depressed, with the index seen at 37 in April, down from 38 in March.

10:30 AM EIA Petroleum Status Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on petroleum inventories in the U.S., whether produced here or abroad. 

1:45 PM Michelle Bowman Speaks

Federal Reserve Vice Chair Michelle Bowman participates in a conversation on “Banking Regulation” before the Institute of International Finance Global Outlook Forum: "Deciphering Risks, Defining Opportunities". 

2:00 PM Beige Book

This book is produced roughly two weeks before the monetary policy meetings of the Federal Open Market Committee.

data.title: SoftBank lenders ask more banks to join $40B OpenAI loan: report
data.description:
• More banks are reportedly being invited to join SoftBank Group Corp.’s (SFTBY) $40B loan backing its investment in US tech giant OpenAI (OPENAI), in one of the biggest tests yet of creditor sentiment toward the Japanese conglomerate’s debt-fueled push further into artificial intelligence.
  • The deal has recently entered a so-called “soft launch” phase, with additional lenders being invited to join as sub-underwriters, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg News.
  • Those interested are required to commit around $5B each, the people said, who asked not to be identified discussing private matters.
  • Last month SoftBank (SFTBY) (SFTBF) said it has secured a $40B bridge loan to bolster investments in OpenAI (OPENAI) and for general corporate ‌purposes.

data.title: Westlake to pay $67M in PVC pipe lawsuit settlement
data.description:
Westlake (WLK) said Tuesday it agreed to pay $67M to settle a class action lawsuit by builders and other direct purchasers alleging price-fixing of PVC pipe and fittings in the U.S. beginning in 2020, in the largest settlement so far in nationwide U.S. antitrust litigation over ‌the PVC pipe market.

In addition to the cash payment, Westlake (WLK) said it will produce documents previously provided to the U.S. ​Department of Justice as part of a separate federal antitrust investigation ​into the ⁠PVC pipe and fittings market.

Westlake (WLK), while denying any wrongdoing, said it will cooperate with the plaintiffs as they pursue related claims in ​the lawsuit against other manufacturers including Atkore (ATKR) and Otter Tail (OTTR).

The proposed settlement, which requires approval by a judge, would provide "significant" compensation to ​thousands of pipe ⁠purchasers, according to plaintiffs’ lawyers.

data.title: Gloo forecasts $190M 2026 revenue while targeting adjusted EBITDA profitability in Q4 2026 following EMD deal
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gloo Holdings (GLOO) Q4 fiscal 2025

Management View

  • "Q4 was a strong quarter for Gloo that exceeded our guidance" (CEO Scott Beck), adding "we more than quadrupled our revenue compared to the prior year period" and "we also exited 2025 with a much stronger balance sheet following our November IPO and the conversion of a significant majority of our debt into equity."
  • "We remain confident in achieving adjusted EBITDA profitability in Q4 2026 and continue to expect to approach adjusted EBITDA profitability in Q3" (CEO Beck), while positioning the strategy as "powering technology and powering reach" and stating, "we're leveraging the latest innovations in agentic AI" to "deliver significantly better outcomes at lower costs, while also creating higher margins for Gloo and highly durable revenue streams."
  • "Today, we announced a definitive agreement to acquire Enterprisemarketdesk, known as EMD, a leading Workday Service Partner" (CEO Beck), and said, "Westfall Group has been immediately accretive since close, and we anticipate EMD will be immediately accretive upon close as well."
  • "Revenue for the quarter was $33.6 million" and "adjusted EBITDA improved $0.7 million sequentially to negative $18.6 million" (CFO Paul Seamon), while highlighting margin and cash with "cost of revenue in the quarter was 76.5%" and "as of January 31, 2026, we had $57.3 million of cash and cash equivalents."

Outlook

  • "We continue to guide to first quarter revenue of $36 million" (CFO Seamon), and "for the quarter, we expect adjusted EBITDA loss to narrow to negative $12 million."
  • "Our full year 2026 revenue outlook is now $190 million, which includes the addition of EMD" (CFO Seamon), adding, "we are confident in our ability to achieve this guidance without any additional acquisitions" and "we continue to expect meaningful sequential improvement each quarter and expect profitability in Q4 2026."
  • Compared with the prior call, "we expect to nearly double revenue in 2026 to over $180 million" and "we are also assuming that $40 million of the $180 million will come from incremental acquisitions" (CFO Seamon, Q3), versus "$190 million" including EMD and "without any additional acquisitions" (CFO Seamon, Q4).

Financial Results

  • "Revenue for the quarter was $33.6 million" (CFO Seamon), with mix details: "platform revenue totaled $20.1 million" and "platform solutions revenue was $13.5 million"; he said sequential performance was supported as "much of the sequential growth was driven by Gloo 360 and Igniter" and "platform solutions revenue was $13.5 million, up 6% sequentially, supported by strong performance from Barna and the addition of Westfall Group."
  • "Cost of revenue in the quarter was 76.5%, an improvement from 83.4% in the prior year period" (CFO Seamon), and "we expect improvement to continue throughout the year."
  • "There are also 2 important noncash items to note that significantly reduced net income in the quarter" (CFO Seamon): "share-based competition was higher than normal due to nonrecurring IPO-related award activity" and "loss from the change in fair value of financial instruments" that "reflects derivative calculations affected by our share price" and "pressured net income and therefore, EPS."

Q&A

  • Richard Baldry, ROTH: asked about "the 2 customers that are nearing $10 million a year in revenue"; Executive Chairman Patrick Gelsinger said "these customers are now taking more of the different offerings of Gloo" and cited bundling "Masterworks, Westfall Gold, Gloo 360, AI" as driving "very large" relationships.
  • Richard Baldry, ROTH: asked whether the "$1 million-plus deals" funnel is changing; Executive Chairman Gelsinger said "it is just getting bigger," "we're also seeing the sales cycle, if anything, shorten," and pointed to replicability in "the university segment" and "the campus ministry segment."
  • Richard Baldry, ROTH: asked how far along AI rollout is and internal adoption; Executive Chairman Gelsinger said AI is "very early" and "we see tremendous opportunity," while CEO Beck added, "AI is actually driving a lot of demand for Gloo 360" because customers "not being able to fully keep up."
  • Yun Suk Kim, Loop: asked if scaling Gloo 360 drives margin improvement; CFO Seamon answered, "Gloo 360 is definitely key each quarter" and tied it to "AI rolled into that."
  • Yun Suk Kim, Loop: asked for EMD revenue and margin profile; Executive Chairman Gelsinger said "we're not giving specific sizes on the deal itself" but emphasized "accretive from day 1" and that "40-plus percent and growing of 360 customers are already using Workday."
  • Matthew Harrigan, Benchmark: asked early reaction to Gloo AI Studio; Executive Chairman Gelsinger said "super early" but noted "early positive anecdotal signals" and that they are tracking "token count rising, API calls, revenues start to materialize."
  • Jason Kreyer, Craig-Hallum: asked about more M&A in fiscal 2026; CEO Beck said there is a "significant pipeline" but "we need no more to be able to hit the numbers that we've got for guidance" and "need no more to be able to get to the EBITDA profitability."
  • Ryan Meyers, Lake Street: asked about cost pressures and recurring revenue; CFO Seamon said "nothing stands out in terms of cost pressures," and CEO Beck said revenue is becoming "locked-in recurring revenue" as Gloo "pull work out" and create "very, very durable" relationships.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, with repeated congratulations and growth-focused probing such as "can you talk about sort of the funnel for $1 million-plus deals" (Richard Baldry, ROTH) and "any additional details" on EMD (Yun Suk Kim, Loop), indicating interest in scale, margins, and disclosure.
  • Management sentiment was positive and confident in guidance and strategy, using high-conviction language including "we remain super confident in our strategy and our outlook for 2026" (CEO Beck) and "we remain focused on progressing towards adjusted EBITDA profitability in Q4" (CFO Seamon).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s “first earnings call as a public company” framing (CEO Beck, Q3), the current call leaned more execution- and profitability-focused, centered on "more than 30% improvement in adjusted EBITDA from Q4" in Q1 guidance (CEO Beck) and accretive M&A integration.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • M&A shifted from announcing "our definitive agreement to acquire Westfall Gold" (CEO Beck, Q3) to stating "we successfully completed the acquisition of Westfall Group" and announcing the EMD agreement (CEO Beck, Q4).
  • Guidance language moved from "expect revenue in excess of $180 million in fiscal year 2026" (CEO Beck, Q3) to "full year 2026 revenue outlook is now $190 million" (CFO Seamon, Q4), with a tighter M&A stance: "we are confident...without any additional acquisitions" (CFO Seamon, Q4) versus Q3’s "robust and actionable M&A pipeline" and "front half weighted" expectation (CFO Seamon, Q3).
  • Analysts stayed focused on large-customer scaling and sales-cycle dynamics across both calls, while Q4 questions expanded into AI Studio monetization signals and Workday-driven cross-sell mechanics for EMD.

Risks and Concerns

  • "January is seasonally slower for fundraising activity" (CFO Seamon), and Westfall "did not contribute to adjusted EBITDA" in the quarter due to that seasonality.
  • Net income volatility drivers included "loss from the change in fair value of financial instruments" that is "affected by our share price" (CFO Seamon) and elevated noncash "share-based" expense tied to "nonrecurring IPO-related award activity" (CFO Seamon).
  • On costs, management did not cite specific new pressures: "nothing stands out in terms of cost pressures" (CFO Seamon).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a Q4 revenue print of $33.6 million, guided Q1 revenue to $36 million with adjusted EBITDA loss at (-$12) million, and raised full-year 2026 revenue outlook to $190 million including the EMD acquisition, while reiterating a target of adjusted EBITDA profitability in Q4 2026. Leadership repeatedly tied larger enterprise deals and improving margins to scaling Gloo 360, bundling across Masterworks/Westfall/Gloo 360/AI, and expanding applied AI offerings such as Gloo AI Studio, while flagging noncash items and seasonality as key factors shaping GAAP results.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Mama's Creations targets net +2 SKUs in each top 10 account while reiterating double-digit growth for fiscal 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mama's Creations (MAMA) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • CEO Adam Michaels framed fiscal 2026 as transformational, saying, "We grew revenue 39% to $171.7 million, expanded adjusted EBITDA over 50% to $15.4 million, completed a transformative acquisition that nearly doubled our manufacturing footprint and capped the year with a record fourth quarter that saw revenue grow 61% to $54 million."
  • Michaels emphasized integration progress at the acquired Crown 1 Bay Shore facility: "Procurement and logistics are 100% centralized" and "Production has been rebalanced across all 3 facilities to optimize capacity, reduce overtime and improve absorption," adding, "we remain on track to bring that facility in line with our mid- to high 20s gross margin corporate target."
  • Michaels highlighted customer expansion and branded momentum, including Costco, Walmart, Target, and Food Lion, and said, "we have set a strategic goal of adding net plus 2 SKUs or items in each of our top 10 accounts."
  • "Revenue for the fourth quarter of fiscal '26 increased 60.7% to $54 million" (Chief Financial Officer Anthony Gruber).

Outlook

  • Management reiterated growth expectations for fiscal 2027; Michaels told an analyst, "we feel comfortable that double-digit growth will continue to gain meaningful share for the year ahead, absolutely."
  • Michaels cautioned on quarterly cadence versus the annual goal due to lapping prior-year promotions and seasonality, saying he would "keep to our double-digit growth aspiration."
  • On gross margin trajectory, Michaels said, "we're planning for and I feel confident that we will be higher 4 quarters from now than we were this quarter," while also noting variability: "from quarter-to-quarter, depending on what season we're in, we might have a point or 2 dip up or down."
  • On capital spending, Michaels said, "the plan is to spend mid- to high single-digit millions of dollars a year" and tied it to internal funding: "You don't get to spend CapEx if you're not making it from cash flow from operations."

Financial Results

  • Gruber reported fiscal Q4 revenue of $54 million and fiscal-year revenue of $171.7 million, attributing growth to "item expansion at existing customers," "high ROI promotional activities," "initial placements at new customers" and "the acquisition of Crown 1."
  • Gruber said Q4 gross profit was $14 million (25.9% of revenue) and fiscal-year gross profit was $43 million (25.1% of revenue), adding, "The fourth quarter gross margin was impacted by the continued ramp of the Crown 1 facility."
  • Gruber reported Q4 net income of $2.2 million, or $0.05 per diluted share, and fiscal-year net income of $5.3 million, or $0.13 per diluted share.
  • Gruber reported Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $5.5 million and fiscal-year adjusted EBITDA of $15.4 million, and said cash and cash equivalents were $20 million as of January 31, 2026, with total debt at $5.4 million.

Q&A

  • Brian Holland, D.A. Davidson: Asked whether "the double-digit growth outlook holds" for fiscal 2027 and whether any quarters could fall below double digits due to tough lapping (especially Costco); CEO Michaels: "Yes" and said he would "keep to our double-digit growth aspiration" while emphasizing continued share gains.
  • Brian Holland, D.A. Davidson: Asked about M&A readiness post-Crown 1 integration; CEO Michaels: "We want to do acquisitions. We don't need to do acquisitions" and said any deal "has to be accretive to our EPS."
  • Brian Holland, D.A. Davidson: Asked whether retailers are engaging Mama’s as a category adviser; CEO Michaels said retailers are calling, including, "Hey, I'd love for you guys just to come and speak to my leadership team and tell them what you're seeing in the category."
  • Eric Des Lauriers, Craig-Hallum: Asked if the Q4 trade promotion step-up was a one-time Costco/Instacart effect or a new normal; CEO Michaels said, "we will continue to invest the trade as long as our gross margins are in the mid- to high 20s" and described adjusting trade "week-to-week, month-to-month" based on inputs like chicken and freight.
  • Eric Des Lauriers, Craig-Hallum: Asked about fiscal 2027 CapEx; CEO Michaels reiterated "mid- to high single-digit millions of dollars a year" and discussed buying additional MAP equipment to extend shelf life.
  • George Kelly, ROTH: Asked about chicken and beef inflation and pricing response; CEO Michaels said the company has been "successful in getting the price increases in to maintain our margins" with "anywhere from 30 days to 60 days" lag, and noted it is contracted for "close to 70% of our chicken sales" while also citing "a little pressure on freight."
  • George Kelly, ROTH: Asked whether Crown 1 revenue could dip sequentially amid SKU exits; CEO Michaels said, "my hope is that Crown is actually flat for the year," expecting SKU exits to be offset by adds while improving margins.
  • Ryan Meyers, Lake Street: Asked if Q4 gross margin is a new baseline; CEO Michaels pointed to commodity and promotion seasonality but said, "I feel confident that we will have -- our gross margins will be higher a year from now than they are today."
  • Ryan Meyers, Lake Street: Asked for branded vs. private-label mix and demand; CEO Michaels said, "I'm seeing a lot more branded" and cited new wins as branded-led, adding, "the price is the same price" for branded vs. private label.
  • Anthony Vendetti, Maxim: Asked on timing of antibiotic-free chicken transition; CEO Michaels said, "100% of what we're purchasing now is NAE chicken."
  • Anthony Vendetti, Maxim: Asked about items per customer and Costco regional expansion; CEO Michaels said "9 of our top 10 customers were either the same, if not more items than they were a year ago," and estimated Costco presence at "3 or 4 regions right this minute" while aiming for broader coverage and new item introductions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive with targeted skepticism on sustainability (lapping, trade, commodities), including Holland’s question on whether quarters could be "less than double digits" and Des Lauriers probing whether trade spend is "a new normal going forward."
  • Management tone was positive and assertive in prepared remarks and mostly steady in Q&A, with Michaels using confident language such as, "we feel comfortable that double-digit growth will continue" and "we will continue to invest the trade," while acknowledging variability from commodities and promotions.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s confidence language appeared more anchored to execution milestones (Costco everyday item status, branded SKU launches), while analyst focus shifted from early acquisition integration mechanics to durability of growth, margins, and spending levels.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q4 emphasized scale outcomes and national retail activation, while Q3 emphasized early Bay Shore integration steps and new-door wins; in Q4, Michaels said Bay Shore integration "has exceeded our expectations," versus Q3’s focus on being "off to a tremendous start."
  • Costco progressed from Q3’s setup for a "first national Costco MVM" to Q4’s reported milestone: "we were informed that we achieved everyday item status in the Northeast."
  • Financially, Q4 introduced full-year results and a larger quarter; Q3 highlighted acquisition-related one-time costs ("$1 million in nonrecurring transaction expenses") and early trade/marketing investment context, while Q4 framed continued investment alongside higher profit and cash.

Risks and Concerns

  • Michaels cited input and logistics volatility, including "beef" staying "relatively stable high," chicken rising seasonally, and "a little pressure on freight," with mitigation via pricing actions, contracting ("close to 70%" of chicken), forecasting tools, and operational efficiency.
  • Michaels flagged the need to manage promotional and comparison risk (especially Costco lapping) and reiterated that quarterly gross margin can fluctuate due to "seasonality of chicken and beef prices" and "promotions and rotations."
  • On M&A, Michaels set a discipline constraint—"it has to be accretive to our EPS"—and framed timing around integration readiness and pricing.

Final Takeaway

Management portrayed fiscal 2026 as a step-change year driven by the Crown 1 acquisition, accelerating club and mass momentum (including Costco everyday status in the Northeast), and expanded branded distribution with multiple national retailers. For fiscal 2027, management reiterated a double-digit growth aspiration, guided to continued but actively managed trade investment tied to mid-to-high-20s gross margin aims, planned mid-to-high single-digit millions of CapEx funded by operating cash flow, and a specific commercial goal of net +2 SKUs across each of its top 10 accounts, while acknowledging commodity, freight, and lapping-related variability quarter to quarter.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil plunges on potential for renewed U.S.-Iran talks, pressure from naval blockade
data.description:
Crude oil futures fell sharply Tuesday on signs the U.S. and Iran are seeking to arrange a second round of peace talks before an April 7 ceasefire expires next week.

Iran may be under pressure to give talks another try, since its economy is virtually dependent upon oil revenues, and the U.S. Navy has started its blockade of Iranian ports, blocking ships from entering or exiting the Strait of Hormuz for the purpose of commerce with Iran.

The U.S. military said no ships had passed through its blockade in the first 24 hours, and that six vessels had turned around when ordered to do so, after Iran had been exporting ~1.5M bbl/day of oil.

U.S. Central Command, which oversees American military operations in the region, is using more than a dozen warships, over 100 fighter jets and surveillance aircraft, and more than 10K troops to enforce the blockade.

While talk of a resumption in U.S.-Iran talks weighed on oil prices Tuesday, the move lower ignores the ​loss of physical barrels of oil that are not moving, PVM Oil Associates analyst Tamas Varga said.

Attacks on energy infrastructure in the Middle East and Iran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz have led to the largest oil supply disruption in history, the International Energy Agency said in its monthly ​report, with 10.1M bbl/day lost in March.

Tuesday's drop of as much as $10/bbl from the previous session's highs reflects "the possibility of renewed talks that could lead to a nuclear deal that would eventually spur a reopening of the strait," Ritterbusch analysts said in a note. "Even a glimmer of negotiating progress can easily spark a dramatic price decline."

Also, the U.S. will ‌not renew a 30-day waiver of sanctions on Iranian oil at sea that expires this week, and quietly let a similar waiver on sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend, Reuters reported.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery plunged 7.9% to $91.28/bbl, its lowest settlement value in three weeks, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery sank 4.6% to $94.79/bbl.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) fell for a fifth consecutive session to its lowest in nearly 18 months, as a mild weather outlook pointed to sluggish demand and strong inventory builds; front-month Nymex gas for May delivery closed down 1.1% to $2.599/MMBtu, its lowest settlement value since October 29, 2024.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: U.S. behind race with China on building out energy for AI, Constellation CEO says
data.description:
The U.S. is "very behind" China in the race to build up energy to feed artificial intelligence data centers, and if building more power plants as quickly as China is the strategy to keep pace with the AI infrastructure expansion, "we're in some trouble," Constellation Energy (CEG) CEO Joseph Dominguez said Tuesday.

China has built the entirety of the U.S. electric system plus 50% more in terms of capacity since 2010, but the U.S. can catch up if it uses its grid more efficiently, Dominguez said at Semafor World Economy in Washington, D.C., noting the U.S. is now less industrialized, which means power demand is no longer around the clock, compared to China.

"If we could manage the peak energy demands, we [would] have a lot of slack in the system we could take advantage of," the CEO said, adding that American consumers could possibly end up with lower energy prices if the strategy is successful.

Asked about local opposition to data center construction, Dominguez said it is "imperative that we win this... for the defense of the nation and our way of life," adding that the U.S. needs a national policy in place instead of state-by-state approvals for power projects.

"If NIMBYism becomes the reason we lose the AI race, for whatever reason, we're in a whole lot of trouble in this country," he said.

ETF: (XLU)

data.title: U.S. crude stockpiles rose 6.1M barrels last week, API says
data.description:
The American Petroleum Institute reportedly shows a larger-than-expected build of 6.1M barrels of oil in U.S. commercial stockpiles for the week ending April 3, after increasing by 3.7M barrels in the previous week.

Gasoline inventories reportedly show a build of 626K barrels for the week, distillate inventories reportedly show a draw of 3.4M barrels, and inventories at the Cushing storage hub reportedly show a draw of 1.7M barrels.

The Energy Information Administration is scheduled to release its weekly U.S. petroleum supply report on Wednesday; the average of analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast domestic commercial crude stocks would increase by 900K barrels, gasoline inventories were expected to drop by 1.7M barrels, and distillate inventories were expected to decline by 2.1M barrels.

WTI front-month May crude oil (CL1:COM) recently traded at $92.90/bbl, up from Tuesday's $91.28/bbl settlement price.

ETF: (USO)

data.title: Northrop Grumman's unit wins $475M contract modification
data.description:
• Northrop Grumman Systems, a wholly owned subsidiary of Northrop Grumman (NOC), received a contract modification worth $475.3M, increasing the total deal value to $1.31B.
  • The project focuses on developing a Glide Phase Interceptor missile system on an accelerated timeline.
  • The work is expected to be completed by June 2028.
  • The total of $174.1M in funding has been allocated at the time of the award, and the Missile Defense Agency is managing the contract.
